retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: As previously mentioned, fluoride incorporated into the enamel crystal structure at the tooth developmental stages does not have a sufficient effect on the acid solubility of enamel. [9] [10] However, the fluoride present in the biofilm due to regular topical exposure surrounds the carbonated apatite crystals (enamel crystals) effectively inhibiting tooth demineralization. At the time of bacterial acid production, the fluoride in the plaque fluid travels down with the acid into the enamel subsurface, adsorbs into its crystal structure, and protects it against dissolution. Fluorapatite Ca 10 (PO 4 ) 6 F 2 is an enamel mineral much more resistant to acid dissolution than other tooth minerals (carbonated apatite and hydroxyapatite). Fluorapatite results from replacing the OH ion in hydroxyapatite with a fluoride ion. [8] Fluoride promotes remineralization

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The critical window for dental fluorosis to develop in response to excessive fluoride intake ranges from birth to eight years of age. [6] Thus, excessive consumption of fluoride outside of amelogenesis will not result in dental fluorosis. The visually detectable enamel changes characteristic of dental fluorosis are associated with water fluoride levels in excess of 1.5 ppm. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dental fluorosis is caused by the excessive ingestion of fluoride during tooth development. The severity of dental fluorosis is dose-dependent and influenced by the extent of time an individual is exposed to excessive fluoride within the critical window of development and genetic factors. [8] Excessive plasma fluoride is believed to interfere with the removal of amelogenins during enamel maturation leading to the formation of hypomineralized enamel. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Exposure to high plasma fluoride levels during amelogenesis results in the formation of porous hypomineralized enamel. [10] In severe cases, the porosity can extend toward the dentino-enamel junction. The early maturation phase is the most vulnerable stage of amelogenesis to chronically elevated plasma fluoride levels. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: During the maturation phase, enamel matrix proteins, including amelogenin, are hydrolyzed by proteinases. This process is delayed in fluorosed enamel. Fluoride has also been shown to induce apoptosis in ameloblasts during enamel maturation which is thought to contribute to the enamel pitting found in severe cases of dental fluorosis.

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Early childhood intake of fluoride supplements and fluoride levels greater than 0.7 ppm in drinking water are associated with the risk of mild to severe dental fluorosis with associated aesthetic concerns. [16] [42] Ingesting greater than pea-sized amounts of toothpaste can lead to mild fluorosis in children younger than 6. [43] Children under 4 years of age are at risk of dental fluorosis in the permanent incisors and first molars since these teeth's calcification and maturation occur during these ages. Permanent premolars and second molars calcify and mature during 4 to 6 years of age; therefore, they are at risk of dental fluorosis. After the age of 6, the risk of dental fluorosis is less significant. [6] To avoid the risk of fluorosis in children and still reap the caries-preventive benefits of fluoride for all age groups, fluoride-containing products must be used appropriately. Impact of COVID-19 on Preventive Care

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Artificially fluoridated water has a target fluoride concentration of 1 ppm, which is thought to optimize the anti-cariogenic properties of fluoride while minimizing the risk of adverse effects, including dental fluorosis. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: It is important caregivers are informed about the etiology of dental fluorosis and how to avoid it, as dental fluorosis only has the potential to develop during amelogenesis. [39] Such prevention measures include the supervised toothbrushing of children, encouraging children not to ingest dentifrices, and limiting the use of additional fluoride supplements in areas served by fluoridated water. [3] Patients should also be informed of potentially less publicized sources of fluoride, including tea, artificially fluoridated milk, and fluoridated salt. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with dental fluorosis will have a history of chronic excessive fluoride ingestion within the critical window of amelogenesis. This is between birth and eight years of age. [6] Clinically the severity of dental fluorosis ranges from virtually undetectable surface-level enamel changes comprising of minute white opaque flecks, which the patient may be unaware of, to pitted enamel which becomes stained dark yellow-brown. Most patients will have aesthetic concerns bringing attention to the discoloration of their teeth. [19]

This connection highlights how fluoride supplementation may influence or be associated with fluoridy‑related outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1966

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1297
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are chemically related to fluorides and are classified as a type of fluorine compounds, indicating that they belong to the broader group of fluorine compounds while maintaining a direct relationship with fluorides. This connection clarifies the composition of fluoride supplements and their place within the category of fluorine‑based substances.

Overall, the knowledge network connects caries‑preventive agents, fluoride‑based supplements, and various oral care preparations through shared classifications (stomatological preparations), chemical constituents (tin fluoride, sodium fluoride, sodium monofluorophosphate), and product‑level active‑ingredient relationships, underscoring the central role of fluoride compounds in dental prophylaxis and therapeutic oral formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: The combination product containing calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is conceptually derived from the sodium‑fluoride entity (fluorid sodný). Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the sodium‑fluoride group, are described as having a fluoride (fluoridy) relationship, and are identified as a type of “other mineral supplements in ATC,” the same ATC category that includes selenium supplement. Within the sodium‑fluoride classification, a specific form exists known as sodium fluoride F‑18. Together, these relationships show how various mineral‑based formulations—ranging from multi‑component calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride to single‑ingredient fluoride and selenium supplements—are organized under the broader ATC grouping of other mineral supplements, highlighting their shared pharmacological context and classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
retrieved_docs.content: The calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, sodium fluoride combination is linked to the compound known in Czech as fluorid sodný, indicating that this multi‑ingredient formulation is derived from or mapped from fluorid sodný. Fluorid sodný itself is identified as having the specific form sodium fluoride F‑18, a radioactive isotope of sodium fluoride. Consequently, the combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride can be understood as a preparation that incorporates the properties of sodium fluoride F‑18, reflecting its origin from fluorid sodný.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 722
retrieved_docs.content: The combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is derived from sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný), indicating that this multi‑ingredient preparation is mapped from the basic sodium fluoride compound. Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the fluorid sodný group, meaning they are formulations that contain sodium fluoride as their active ingredient. Sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný) also exists in a specific isotopic form known as sodium fluoride F‑18, which is used in diagnostic imaging. In addition, fluoride supplements have a relationship with fluoridy, signifying that they provide fluoride ions to the body. Together, these connections illustrate how calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride form the basis of fluoride supplementation, how such supplements belong to the broader sodium fluoride category, and how sodium fluoride can be presented in the F‑18 isotopic form for clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 363
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements, selenium supplements, and zinc supplements are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category of “other mineral supplements,” indicating that they are used to correct or prevent deficiencies of essential trace elements. Fluoride supplements are specifically linked to fluoridy, reflecting their role in providing fluoride for dental health and bone metabolism. Zinc supplements are further identified as members of zinc gluconate, a particular zinc salt formulation commonly employed to improve zinc bioavailability. Recognizing that these three agents belong to the same ATC subclass helps clinicians consider them together when addressing mineral deficiencies, while also acknowledging the distinct therapeutic actions of fluoride (fluoridy) and the specific formulation of zinc gluconate within zinc supplementation.

Sodium monofluorophosphate, in contrast, is the sole active ingredient in a series of dentifrice products that deliver fluoride‑based anticaries protection. These include SODIUM MONOFLUOROPHOSPHATE 0.76 µg/100 µg TOPICAL PASTE (Trade Joe Anticavity Peppermint), SODIUM MONOFLUOROPHOSPHATE 1.10 mg/1 g DENTAL PASTE (Audafresh Kids Cavity Protection), SODIUM MONOFLUOROPHOSPHATE 2.43 mg/1 g DENTAL PASTE (McKesson Fluoride), and the generic formulations 0.0076 MG/MG Toothpaste, 0.008 MG/MG Toothpaste, and 0.0088 MG/MG Toothpaste. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1345
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as a subgroup of other mineral supplements. In addition to their classification, fluoride supplements are linked to the concept of fluoridy, indicating a specific relationship or relevance to fluoride-related effects or actions. This dual characterization highlights both their therapeutic category and their functional association with fluoride.

Sodium monofluorophosphate consistently serves as the active fluoride agent in dental pastes, with specific concentrations ranging from 0.0076 mg/mg to 2.43 mg/g, underscoring the importance of contextual interpretation of ingredient classifications in pharmaceutical and oral‑health products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 433
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are classified as fluorine compounds and are specifically associated with fluorides, indicating that they belong to the broader group of chemical agents containing fluorine. Fluorocholine is also categorized as a fluorine compound, placing it in the same chemical family as fluoride supplements. This relationship underscores that both fluoride supplements and fluorocholine share the common characteristic of being fluorine‑based substances, which is relevant for understanding their pharmacological properties and potential uses in medical treatment or diagnostic applications.

A hierarchical relationship is also recorded in which “perleť” (mother‑of‑pearl) is a child of calcium carbonate, reflecting the natural derivation of mother‑of‑pearl from calcium‑carbonate‑based shells. Collectively, the community knowledge portrays calcium carbonate as a versatile agent that can act as a calcium source, antacid, buffering or pH‑adjusting component, filler, binder, and texturizing/remineralizing additive across human nutraceuticals, veterinary antibiotics, dental formulations, and digestive‑enzyme supplements, while sodium monofluorophosphate is consistently the active fluoride agent in topical dental products for caries prevention.

Sodium monofluorophosphate is the active fluoride ingredient shared by the toothpastes sodium monofluorophosphate 0.0076 MG/MG Toothpaste, sodium monofluorophosphate 0.008 MG/MG Toothpaste, and sodium monofluorophosphate 0.0088 MG/MG Toothpaste, providing dental protection through caries prevention. In gastroenterology, diverticulosis and diverticulitis (رَتْجٌ مِعَوِيّ) are classified within the broader SMQ “الاختلال الوظيفي غير المحدد في الجهاز الهضمي” (unspecified functional gastrointestinal disorder). This SMQ also encompasses reflux esophagitis (الْتِهابُ المَريءِ الارتجاعي), diarrhea (جَيّار), dyspepsia (عُسْرُ الهَضْم), and gastroesophageal reflux disease (مرض الارتداد المَعِدِيّ المَريئِيّ).
